2x4-frame-panel-chest-of-drawers.returnReason: REJECT: step 4 contradicts its own joinery - two 15-1/2in sides BETWEEN a 16in front and back gives 16in wide x 17in deep, not the stated 17-1/2in x 15-1/2in. The slide clearance in the next sentence is computed off the wrong figure (real result 1-3/4in per side, not 1in), and the listed 15-1/2in x 16in bottom fits neither reading. The back-panel defect it reports is real and honestly stated.

4x4-truss-bench.returnReason: REJECT: step 5 uses the 65in CLEAR SPAN as the base footprint. The 65in stretcher sits between the leg ends, so the base is 65 + two 3-1/2in = 72in and the overhang is 7-1/2in per end, not the stated 11in.

flags: Stringer spacing is not given anywhere, and neither is where along the stringers the legs sit. The rewrite gives the checks (even overhang at both ends, no rock on a flat floor) instead of inventing numbers.

COMPUTED, not authored: five 3-1/2" top boards plus four 1/4" gaps covers 18-1/2" of the stringers' 23-1/2" length, so the position of that block of decking along the stringer is a decision the builder has to make. The 1/4" gap itself is a derived suggestion for drainage.
COMPUTED: 2x4 yield is comfortable — two 23-1/2" stringers plus two 13-3/4" legs is 74-1/2" of a 96" board. 1x4: five 22-1/2" boards is 112-1/2" of a 144" board.
The original step 2 offers 'nails or screws', but no nails appear in materials[]. Prose uses the listed exterior screws only.
No sander is in tools[]; finishing is hand sanding by implication.

MATERIAL SHORTFALL (1x3): the cut list needs 4 side trims @ 15-3/4", one 49" top trim, 2 door stiles @ 54-1/4", 2 door rails @ 14", 2 face-frame stiles @ 23-1/2" and 2 rails @ 18" = 331-1/2" of 1x3. The material list supplies three 8 ft boards = 288". Roughly a board and a half short.

MATERIAL SHORTFALL (1x2): the cut list needs 4 bottom drawer trims @ 44", 4 smaller drawer trims @ 23-1/4" and one 49" top trim = 318". The material list supplies two 8 ft boards = 192". Close to two boards short.
CONTRADICTION: step 1 as written says 'attach the 1x2 side trim', but the cut list's 'Side trim, top and bottom' is 2-1/2" wide from Pine 1x3. The rewrite says 1x3.

CONTRADICTION: the large drawer's cut-list labels are reversed. Its bottom is 16" x 43", and 41-1/2" + two 3/4" pieces = 43" — so the 41-1/2" parts must be the front and back and the 16" parts the sides, giving a 43"-wide x 16"-deep box that suits the 44" opening. As labeled ('sides' 41-1/2", 'front/back' 16") the box would be 16" wide and 43" deep. The rewrite states the finished box size rather than the labels.

Under-specified: the legs are 69" and the side panels 67". The plan never says how the 2" difference registers (all at the bottom as feet, all at the top, or split). The rewrite tells the builder to reference both assemblies off the same end and stops there.
Under-specified: the bottom shelf's height above the floor is given nowhere, so the drawer-stack height is undetermined.

Under-specified: the four 44" bottom drawer trims — the plan does not say where all four go (front and back, above and below the shelf). The rewrite says front and back edges and flags the ambiguity rather than inventing a layout.

DERIVED: small drawer openings. Faces are 9-1/4" x 23" with a stated 1/8" gap all round, so the openings must finish 9-1/2" x 23-1/4" — and 23-1/4" is exactly the smaller-drawer-trim length, which corroborates it.

Tight but sound: the mirror at 49-1/4" x 14" is exactly the door frame's opening (54-1/4" stiles less two 2-1/2" rails = 49-1/4"; 14" rail length). Zero clearance — noted in the prose as 'exactly its size'.
Minor: 'Knobs or handles' quantity 7 against 4 drawers and 1 door.

MATERIAL SHORTFALL for the corner add-on, checked by packing. All ten 1x2 parts total 228-1/2" and need three 8 ft boards (best packing: 94-3/4" + 88-3/4" + 22-1/2"); only two (192") are listed. The main standalone unit alone uses 169", which the two supplied boards do cover — so the listed quantity is right for the standalone build and short by one board for the corner add-on. Stated that way rather than as a flat defect.

The two back-panel widths look transposed: 19" is assigned to the main unit and 31-1/2" to the corner add-on, but the main unit is the wider of the two (29-1/4" shelves versus 16-1/2"). The rewrite tells the builder to measure the real openings.

COMPUTED: main case is 30-3/4" wide over the outside (29-1/4" shelves plus two 3/4" side panels), so the 32-3/4" top overhangs 1" at each end. The add-on's 18-1/2" top over 16-1/2" shelves does not give the same overhang — check before cutting.
Shelf heights are not dimensioned anywhere; nothing states where the middle shelf sits on either section.
Yields verified with no shortfall: 1x12 needs 222-1/4" of 240" and packs into two 10 ft boards; 2x2 needs 357" of 384" and packs into four 8 ft boards.

MATERIAL SHORTFALL (known defect, confirmed): two front/back pieces at 38-1/2" plus three pieces at 15-1/2" = 123-1/2" of 1x4, against the single 10 ft (120") board the material list supplies — about 3-1/2" short before any saw kerf. Surfaced in the cut step rather than written around.

Under-specified BY DESIGN: the divider position is deliberately left to the builder's own changing pad, and every cut-list row carries the note 'measure your changing pad and adjust as needed'. The rewrite keeps that and does not invent a divider location.

DERIVED: with the two 15-1/2" ends captured between the 38-1/2" front and back, the box finishes 38-1/2" x 17" outside with a 15-1/2" interior depth. Not stated in the plan; used only as a check, not as a build number.
Safety-relevant gap: the plan states plainly that nothing in the frame secures it to the cabinet. Kept prominent in the rewrite because the failure mode is a topper sliding with a baby on it.

basic-play-toy-workbench.returnReason: REJECT: step 2 stands the 11-1/2in elevation strips on the 18in LONG edges; they belong on the 11-1/4in end edges, which is what its own step 1 (18in + 3/4in per side = 19-1/2in) and step 4 both require. As written the assembly is 12in wide, not 19-1/2in. Also step 1s causal claim about the legs is invented - 19-1/2in there is a leg HEIGHT.

flags: The 1x3 material note reads 'Guardrail top/bottom rails; mattress slats', but all four listed 1x3 guardrail rails (75", 72", 63-1/2", 60-1/2") each need their own 8 ft board — all four supplied boards are consumed and no 1x3 is left for slats. Slats have to come from the separate 'Bunkie board or slat lumber' line.
No cut-list part exists for mattress slats at all — no length, no count. Spacing is deferred to the mattress maker, which is reasonable, but the quantity is not buyable from the list.

SAFETY-RELEVANT GAP: guardrail height above the mattress is never stated. The twenty-eight 8" 1x2 rails set it implicitly, but nothing says how many go on each of the two guardrails (which are different lengths), so an even split is not safe to assume.

COMPUTED: no material shortfall anywhere. 1x6 needs 228" of 240"; 2x2 needs 324" of 384"; 1x2 needs 450-1/2" of 576" and packs into six boards; the two 21-3/4" x 39" panels sit side by side in 43-1/2" of the 48" half sheet.
Cleat height on the side rail is not dimensioned; 'flush to the bottom edge' is inferred from the guardrail needing to keep its height above the mattress.

PARTITION PLACEMENT IS NOT DIMENSIONED, and equal spacing does not work. COMPUTED: three equal bays across the 54-1/2" interior would each be about 17-2/3", which is narrower than the 20-1/4" drawer boxes. The self-consistent reading is a wider centre bay (roughly 20-1/2") for the two stacked drawers with a door bay of about 16-1/8" on each side, which also fits the 15-3/8" door rails. Derived, not authored.

'Drawer sides' are 11-1/2" and 'Drawer bottoms' are 11-1/4" (the 1x12's actual width) — a 1/4" mismatch. Both parts carry a 'cut to the width of your 1x12' note, so it is a fit-to-stock instruction rather than a hard error.
The 2x2 and 1x2 material notes both list 'drawer guides', but no guide part appears in the cut list. Guides are cut to fit from offcuts.
COMPUTED: interior height is 18-3/4" (19-1/2" sides less the 3/4" top), which matches the 18-3/4" partitions exactly — that is the check that the box is going together right.

CONTRADICTION: the four bookshelf shelves are 46-3/8", but the bookshelf top is 92" and a 3/4" center panel splits it, so each bay is about 44-7/8". Two 46-3/8" shelves cannot fit inside a 92" case. The rewrite tells the builder to measure each bay and trim, and does not repeat 46-3/8" as a build number.
CONTRADICTION: the shelf trim is listed at 43" for shelves listed at 46-3/8". The row is marked 'cut to fit', so the rewrite treats it as cut-to-fit and says so.

UNEXPLAINED 2-1/4" OFFSET, twice: bookshelf side panels 24" vs center panel 26-1/4", and desk outer leg panel 27" vs inside leg panel 29-1/4". The plan never says where the extra 2-1/4" goes. The rewrite registers every panel to the underside of the tabletop frame and flags the difference rather than inventing a placement.

MISSING CUT-LIST ENTRY: step 6 calls for '1x2 cleats' on the inside of the desk leg; no cleat row exists. There is spare 1x2 (398" of parts against 480" supplied), so it is a cut-list gap, not a material shortfall.

DERIVED (exact, and it validates the frame parts): the 95" bookshelf side frame = the 92" plywood top plus a 1-1/2"-thick end frame at each end, and the 70" desk side frame = the 67" desk top plus the same. Used in the prose as the fit check.
Material check, no defect: two plywood sheets ripped to 15-3/4" give six 96" strips (576") against 475" of parts, and 5 of the 6 ripped 2x4s cover 591" of frame and leg parts. Both sufficient.
Deliberate, not a defect: the desk legs and outer leg panel are cut from the measured height of the assembled bookshelf, not from the listed 27". The rewrite keeps that as the governing instruction.

braden-entryway-hutch.returnReason: REJECT: step 6 says to trim the 27-1/2in shelf trim to fit a 28-1/2in opening and nail it to the side front edges - it is 1in SHORT and reaches neither side. Coherent only if the 49-1/4in side trims go in FIRST, but the new step 8 installs them last on the same edges, so the two collide.

built-in-daybed-frame.returnReason: 2-1 confirm OVERRIDDEN: step 4 needs a platform deeper than 24-1/2in while steps 1-2 hand over the cut lists naming unchallenged, making the depth 10-1/2in. The patch calls this unresolvable, but the data resolves it - the description targets a 75in span for a twin (38 x 75), so the 75in slats run the mattress length and the 37-3/4in piece is the depth member.

flags: MATERIAL SHORTFALL (verified by packing, not just by total): the 1x12 parts are 72", 69-1/2", two at 35-1/4", two at 34-3/8" and one at 31-1/2" = 312-1/4" against four 8 ft boards (384"). It still cannot be cut from four boards: the 72" and 69-1/2" parts each consume a board alone (leaving 24" and 26-1/2"), and the remaining five parts pack at most two per board. Five 1x12s are required.

CONTRADICTION: the cut list's doors (15-1/2" x 15", four of them) do not reconcile with the face frame. A 71" header less four 2-1/2" stiles leaves 61" across, and 32-3/4" stiles less a 2-1/2" header and footer leaves 27-3/4" of height — neither divides into four 15-1/2" x 15" doors. The plan already says to size doors to the actual openings, so the rewrite builds on that and quotes no door dimension.
CONTRADICTION: face frame center divider trim is 27-1/4", but the opening between a 2-1/2" header and footer inside 32-3/4" stiles is 27-3/4". Half an inch out.
MISSING MATERIAL: the doors are specified as a 1x3 frame with no panel, glass or insert of any kind in materials[] or the cut list — a frame with nothing in the middle.
CONTRADICTION: base moulding is listed as four 20" pieces, which cannot wrap a 71"-wide cabinet. The single 8 ft stick can (71" front plus two returns), which is what the rewrite says.
DERIVED: the base moulding returns are about 11-1/4", the carcass depth taken from the 1x12 cut-list width; 71" + two 11-1/4" returns = 93-1/2" out of a 96" stick.
DERIVED: nail spacing for the back panel (about every 6") is a general practice figure, not from the plan.

Sound and used as a check: the 72" top over a 71" case gives a 1/2" overhang at each end, and 71" less two 3/4" sides is the 69-1/2" bottom shelf. The 71" x 36" back also equals the 35-1/4" sides plus the 3/4" top.

MATERIAL SHORTFALL (hardware cloth): the material list supplies 4 ft of 36"-wide cloth, i.e. a 36" x 48" piece. The steps require covering the mesh floor (about 36" x 15-1/2"), the underside of the middle shelf (the same), and the back (the panel is 38-1/2" x 60"). The floor and shelf alone consume most of it; the back cannot be covered.

MATERIAL SHORTFALL (1x2): the cut list needs four side trims and three grated-bottom supports at 15-1/2" plus two top trims at 39" = 186-1/2" against two 8 ft boards (192"). That leaves 5-1/2", while the cut list's own note says to 'add more than three' bottom supports if the mesh needs it — there is no stock for a fourth.

TIGHT TO THE POINT OF FAILURE (plywood): the three 15-1/2" strips must yield two 60" sides, three 36" shelves and a 39" top. Two strips have to carry 60" + 36" = 96", exactly the strip length, with zero allowance for saw kerf. Surfaced in the cut step with a cutting order that puts the kerf into the shelves.
CONTRADICTION: the back panel is 38-1/2" wide against a carcass that measures 37-1/2" across (36" between the sides plus two 3/4" panels) — an inch too wide.

Under-specified: the legs are 66" and the side panels 60"; the plan never says how the 6" difference is distributed. The description says the cabinet stands off the ground, so the rewrite keeps the leg tops flush with the panel tops and names that as a reading of the description, not a stated dimension.
Under-specified: no shelf heights are given anywhere, so the three shelves' vertical positions are the builder's call. The rewrite does not invent them.

DERIVED: carcass outside width 37-1/2" (36" trim/shelf length + two 3/4" side panels), and hence the 39" top overhangs 3/4" at each side. Also derived: two doors at 17-3/4" wide each (12-3/4" rail + two 2-1/2" stiles) sit side by side in the 36" opening.
Note, no defect: 2x2 stock is exactly sufficient — four 66" legs (one per board) and eight 36" trims (two per board) fill all eight boards. The 1x8 at 3 ft barely covers the 35-3/4" cleanout door.

compact-outdoor-dining-table.returnReason: REJECT: step 1 states an impossible footprint - 47-3/4in x 27-1/2in requires BOTH the sides and the ends to overlap each other. Step 2 settles it (ends between sides = 44-3/4in x 27-1/2in), so step 1s "which is exactly why the deck boards are 47-3/4in" is wrong by 3in and step 6s flush deck boards overhang 1-1/2in per end. The 2x2 restraint was correct - no shortfall exists.

flags: Where the two rails sit on each side frame — shelf height and top rail height — is not dimensioned anywhere in the plan.

COMPUTED: the base is 21-1/2" square (18-1/2" rails plus two 1-1/2" legs) and the top is 21-3/4" x 22-1/4", so the reveal is only 1/8" on two sides and 3/8" on the other two. It is not an even overhang and it must be measured, not eyeballed.
COMPUTED: five 1-1/2" slats plus four authored 1-7/8" gaps spans 15" against 18-1/2" outside pieces. The plan does not say how the remaining 3-1/2" is distributed at the two ends.
COMPUTED: 1x8 yield is tight — three 22-1/4" boards is 66-3/4" of a 72" board.

Part allocation verified against the plan's own step text: the thirteen 18-1/2" pieces split 4 (side frames) + 7 (shelf: two outside pieces plus five slats) + 2 (base), which is exactly 13. The 2x2 packs into the four 8 ft boards listed (333-1/2" of 384").

QUANTITY DEFECT: 'Drawer sides' quantity 6 at 12-1/4". Six drawers need twelve sides. The front/back row is correctly 12. Correcting it also creates a MATERIAL SHORTFALL: 12 sides (147") + 12 fronts/backs (204") = 351" against the 288" of 1x10 supplied ('1x10, 24 ft total'). As listed (6 sides) it totals 277-1/2" and just fits.
QUANTITY DEFECT: 'Face frame mid uprights' quantity 4. Two identical face frames, each with two rows of three openings, need two uprights per row per frame = 8. Four covers one frame only.

CONTRADICTION: the drawer bottoms are 17-3/4" x 13-1/4", which matches neither assembly order of the 17" fronts/backs and 12-1/4" sides (those give either 17" x 13-3/4" or 18-1/2" x 12-1/4"). The rewrite tells the builder to size each box to its opening, which the plan already requires, rather than quoting a box dimension.

DERIVED (exact): opening size. (43-1/2" less two 1-1/2" mid uprights) / 3 = 13-1/2" wide, and the mid uprights are 11-1/2" tall. The 11-1/4" x 13-1/4" drawer faces then land at exactly the stated 1/8" gap all round — which confirms the face's 13-1/4" is its width and 11-1/4" its height.
DERIVED (exact): the top is six 3-1/2" boards = 21" wide, which is exactly the 21" top end board length.
DERIVED: three 5-1/2" side panels = 16-1/2", exactly the connecting-rail length — corroborating the cut list's own note that the rails 'should match the width of three 1x6s'.

cube-cpu-cart.returnReason: 3-0 REJECT: step 1 and step 4 state DIFFERENT plywood shortfalls (two panels short vs one) - the one number that governs how much to buy. Also the 12in long shelf and third 19-1/2in partition are cut in step 1 and never installed; the description promises the dropped shelf.

diy-parsons-small-coffee-table.returnReason: REJECT: step 4 needs two more 22-1/2in 2x2 rails; the cut list has exactly four and step 3 already attaches all four to the panel underside. Also the supports are run perpendicular to the glue seams, but the seams run the 22-1/2in direction so the perpendicular span is 45in.

diy-play-kitchen-back-wall.returnReason: REJECT: step 2 puts the oven bay BELOW the bottom shelf, step 4 puts it ABOVE. The two readings give bottom-shelf heights of ~14-1/2in vs 2-1/2in. Step 4 also checks the 11-1/4in door against an 11-1/2in width but never against the 17in bay height - 2-3/4in out on the axis step 5 promises an even reveal on.

doll-high-chair.returnReason: 3-0 REJECT: three incompatible widths asserted as consistent - 6in back-slat supports, 9in slats and tray, 10-1/2in seat. None flagged. Also the supports are seated by their "angled ends" (plural) when the cut list gives each ONE 10deg end.

flags: DESCRIPTION VS CUT LIST, two readings — flagged, not resolved. The summary and description describe two side-by-side backrests, but the cut list's four 43-1/2" backrest supports and eleven 30" slats fit ONE full-width backrest across the 44-1/2" bay. COMPUTED: eleven 3-1/2" slats = 38-1/2", plus ten 1/2" gaps = 43-1/2" exactly, which matches the support length to the inch. Two backrests would each be about 22" wide and could not use 43-1/2" supports.
'Prop-up spacers' quantity is 3, an odd number for a paired mechanism, and step 5's 'remaining prop-up piece' is not a cut-list part at all.
Six hinges are listed but the plan never says how many go on the backrest versus the prop-ups.
Where the backrest bay sits along the 76-1/2" frame is not dimensioned; the 27" cleats imply a 27"-deep bay (computed).

COMPUTED and verified exact: the 1x4 lands on precisely the thirteen boards listed — two boards for the 79-1/2" long deck pieces, then one 49-1/2" centre piece plus one 30" slat from each of eleven boards, leaving the roughly 16-1/2" offcuts the description mentions (96" less 49-1/2" less 30"). 2x4 (428-1/2" of 672") and 2x2 (240" of 288") both pack comfortably.
COMPUTED: frame finishes 76-1/2" long by 47-1/2" wide over the outside faces, which is what gives the 79-1/2" deck pieces a 1-1/2" overhang at each end and the 49-1/2" centre pieces 1" at each side.

MATERIAL SHORTFALL (1/4" sheet): the back is 89-1/2" x 39-1/2" and the three drawer bottoms (12-1/2" x 31") are assigned to the same single sheet. Cutting the back out of a 48" x 96" sheet leaves only an 8-1/2"-wide strip and a 6-1/2" strip — neither takes a 12-1/2"-wide bottom. A second piece of 1/4" material is needed.
QUANTITY DEFECT: 'Door frames' is 2 pieces at 34". A frame-and-panel door needs four frame members, so two doors need eight. The row is marked cut-to-fit, which covers the length but not the count.

CONTRADICTION: door panels are listed at 25" x 28", but the end (door) openings work out to about 23" wide — the 89-1/2" face frame less four 2-1/2" stiles is 79-1/2", less the 33-1/2" drawer bay leaves 46" split between two openings. A 25"-wide panel cannot go in a 23" door.

CONTRADICTION (description vs. data): the description says 'two internal dividers form four bays'. Two dividers make three bays, which is what the four face-frame stiles and the single 33-1/2" drawer bay actually describe — a drawer bay in the middle and a door bay at each end.
CONTRADICTION: the dividers are 34" while the interior height between the 88" top and bottom panels is 38" (39-1/2" sides less two 3/4" panels). The plan does not say what fills the remaining 4".

CONTRADICTION: drawer bottoms are 12-1/2" x 31", which matches neither assembly order of the 31" fronts/backs and 14" sides. The rewrite tells the builder to size the boxes to the opening and their slides, which the cut list already marks as cut-to-fit.

DERIVED (exact, used as fit checks): outside width 89-1/2" (88" panels captured between two 3/4" sides), matching the back and the face frame; face frame height 39-1/2" (2-1/2" + 31-1/2" + 5-1/2"), matching the sides; top planking 17-1/2" wide (five 3-1/2" boards) over a 16-1/2" case-plus-face-frame depth, so about 1/2" of overhang front and back, and 3/4" at each end from the 91" length.
DERIVED: the moulding run is about 122-1/2" (89-1/2" front plus two 16-1/2" returns) against the 12 ft of each moulding supplied — sufficient.
Note, no defect: the 3/4" plywood is sufficient — six 96" strips (576") against 429" of parts, and it packs cleanly.

farmhouse-media-console-with-doors.returnReason: 2-1 confirm OVERRIDDEN: the face-frame shortfall is directionally right but the magnitude is wrong. The 21-1/4in x 54-3/4in back panel and the 16in doors both prove the sides flank the bottom, making the case 54in wide - so the longest listed frame part is 3in short, not "about 1-1/2in", and step 7s end-gap figures are wrong twice over. The three lumber findings are correct.

flags: 'Storage box backs' are listed at 75" x 75". The box's own parts make it 15" tall (13-1/2" verticals plus two 3/4" horizontals), so the back should be about 75" x 15"; and two 75" x 75" panels cannot come from the single 4x8 sheet of 1/4" plywood listed. Checked twice. On the corrected 75" x 15" reading the sheet does cover both backs plus the six 23" x 14" drawer bottoms (4,182 sq in of 4,608), so only the 75" figure is wrong — there is no 1/4" plywood shortfall.

'Drawer box sides' (21-1/2") and 'Drawer box front/back' (14") appear transposed. The 23" x 14" drawer bottom and the 14" Euro slides both say the 14" runs front to back and the 21-1/2" spans between the sides (23" less two 3/4" sides). The lengths are self-consistent; only the labels are swapped.

MATERIALS WITH NO CUT-LIST PART: '1x3, 8 ft' quantity 9 and '2x2, 8 ft' quantity 3 appear in materials[] and in no cut-list row at all. The 2x6 note refers to 'part of the slat system', so these are presumably the mattress slats and cleats — but nothing sizes, counts or places them. This is the largest gap in the plan.

COMPUTED and matching the plan's own check: each storage box opening is 24" wide (75" less four 3/4" verticals, divided into three bays), which is exactly the '24" wide' figure step 4 tells you to verify.
COMPUTED: box height 15", depth 15-3/4"; drawer face 23-3/4" x 13-1/4" in a 24" x 13-1/2" opening gives the even 1/8" gap the plan asks for.
Euro slides are listed as unit 'each', quantity 6, for six drawers; slides sell in pairs, so confirm whether that means six pairs.
COMPUTED and verified exact: 1x10 needs 426" and packs into precisely the five 8 ft boards listed. 4x4 posts, 1x4, 2x4 and 2x6 all have margin.

COMPUTED: the two 3/8" plywood parts are 16-1/2" and 31-1/2" wide, which is exactly 48" — the full width of the sheet — with no allowance for the saw kerf between them. One part will finish a blade-width narrow unless you plan for it.

COMPUTED: 1x4 legs are extremely tight. Six legs at 31-3/4" is 190-1/2" of the 192" supplied, and three per board is 95-1/4" of a 96" board before kerf. Not a stated shortfall, but there is no margin at all.
The front legs are described as 'a paired A-frame style' but no splay angle, leg spacing, or position along the frames is given anywhere.

TWO READINGS, not resolved: the 1x8 'Top edge trim' (71" x 7-1/4") could lie flat beside the 1x12 top, making an 18-1/2"-deep top over a 17"-wide frame, or stand as a front skirt. Both are consistent with the 71" length. The rewrite names both and defers to the photo.
COMPUTED and verified: 2x4 is fine — 334" of parts against 384", and one 66-1/2" long rail plus one 17" short rail fits each 96" board exactly four times over. No shortfall.
COMPUTED: the frames finish 69-1/2" long by 17" wide, matching the 69-1/2" tray liner length; the 71" top is 69-1/2" of frame plus 3/4" at each end.
Overall height and the gap between the two frames are never dimensioned; only the 31-3/4" leg length is given.

flip-down-wall-art-desk.returnReason: REJECT: step 2 fits the 11-1/4in and 12-3/4in pieces between the 24in top and bottom, whose clear gap is 14-1/2in by the steps own derivation. They are 3-1/4in and 1-3/4in short. The source said only "assemble starting in the center"; the placement was invented against its own arithmetic.

flags: The two 69" side aprons are in the cut list but appear in NONE of the plan's original steps — the aprons step places only the end aprons. The rewrite places them.

TWO READINGS on overhang, not resolved. If the 69" side aprons run outside and the 30-1/4" end aprons sit between them, the frame is 69" x 33-1/4" and the 72" x 36-1/4" top overhangs an even 1-1/2" all round. The original step text ('end aprons on top, on the outsides of the leg sets') suggests the opposite arrangement, which would give a 72" frame length and no end overhang at all. Both are internally consistent; the even-overhang reading is stated as the check, with the alternative named.
The five 2x8 tabletop boards are 72", the full nominal length of the 6 ft boards listed — no trim allowance.
COMPUTED, not authored: tabletop support spacing of about 11-1/2" with one support at each end (seven supports, six bays across 69").
COMPUTED: the top is 36-1/4" wide pulled tight (five 7-1/4" boards), or 37-1/4" with the exterior-build 1/4" drainage gaps — allow for that when centring the frame.
Stretcher heights (how far down the lower end stretcher sits) are not dimensioned anywhere.
COMPUTED and verified: 2x4 parts total 523-1/2" and pack into exactly the six 8 ft boards listed; 2x2 needs 211-3/4" of 288". No shortfall.
The original final step tagged 'Wood glue' as its material, which is not used in finishing; corrected to no material.

hutch-open-shelf-dresser.returnReason: 2-1 confirm OVERRIDDEN: step 7 crown layer 2 is a zero step-out. 63-1/2in cap less the 60-1/2in case = 3in = four 3/4in side thicknesses, so layer 2 must sit OUTBOARD of layer 1; as written the cap overhangs its returns 3/4in per end with nothing behind it. All three verifiers saw the wording; only one worked the consequence.

flags: 1x3 IS EFFECTIVELY SHORT (checked by bin-packing twice). Parts total 644-3/4" against 672" in the seven 8 ft boards listed. It does close on paper — the best packing found is 90-1/2" + 96" + 95" + 95" + 92" + 90-1/2" + 85-3/4" — but one board has to be cut end to end at exactly 96" with no kerf allowance and two more sit at 95". Practically, buy an eighth 1x3. Stated as tightness with the arithmetic shown rather than as a flat defect.
The description's 'extra 1x2 across the back of the middle support' and the 'middle support' itself are not cut-list parts.
COMPUTED, not authored: eleven 2-1/2" seat slats across the 31-1/2" opening leaves about 4" of total gap — roughly 3/8" between slats.
COMPUTED: the 33" seat front/back less the two 3/4" faces is exactly 31-1/2", the long rail and seat support length — a useful check that the pieces are going where they belong.

MAJOR GAP: the 1x3 arm parts (2 at 15-3/4"), long rails (2 at 31-1/2") and uprights (4 at 18-1/4") are never assigned to a step anywhere in the plan. How the arms and the frame actually go together is not described, and the rewrite could not invent it — it places the uprights and rails by their arithmetic and leaves arm geometry alone.
Overall bench dimensions are never stated; 33" wide is implied by the 33" seat front/back and top rails.
1x2 verified with plenty of margin: 197" of the 288" supplied.

lack-inspired-bookcase.returnReason: 2-1 REJECT: step 3s "four even 18in bays" is false. With 1-1/2in shelves flush at the ends the centers are 17-1/4/18/18/17-1/4 and the clear openings 15-3/4/16-1/2/16-1/2/15-3/4. The 18/36/54 marks are authored and correct; the derived claim is the rewrites own invention. v2 also found 30 screws prescribed against 24 in materials.

flags: The split of the eight 72" 1x6 slats between seat and back is not specified anywhere. COMPUTED: four across the 25-3/4"-deep seat covers 22" and leaves about 3-3/4" of total gap; four on the back covers 22" of the 30-1/4" back support and leaves about 8". Both figures are derived, not authored.
The cut-list part is named 'Seat, back, and arm slats' but the arms are 2x4 arm rests — no slat is ever assigned to an arm.
The eight slats are used at the full 72" length of the '1x6, 72"' boards supplied — zero trim allowance.

COMPUTED and self-consistent: 69" front and back rails between the two 25-3/4" sides give 72" overall, exactly the slat length, so the slats finish flush at both ends. The 22-3/4" centre supports are 25-3/4" less the two 1-1/2" rails, and they divide the 69" span into three bays of 22". Every one of those numbers checks out against another part, which is unusually clean.
COMPUTED: 2x4 parts total 443" and pack into exactly the five 8 ft boards listed — no shortfall.
Back support height above the seat, arm height, and where the hanging hardware goes along the side aprons are not dimensioned; the plan gives the load principle but no positions.

NO 1x3 SHORTFALL — checked, because the totals look close. The ten 1x3 parts come to 266-3/4" against 288" (three 8 ft boards) and they pack: one long leg plus one medium leg on each of two boards (87-1/8" each), and everything else on the third (92-1/2"). Buy three, not four.
The original step 3 calls for '2" finish nails' but the only nail listed is 'Finish nails, 1-1/4"'. Prose corrected to the listed material.
The steps call for glue at every joint but no wood glue appears in materials[] at all.

COMPUTED: the two legs sit 15" apart on the inside (the spacer length), so their centres land about 15-3/4" apart — near, but not equal to, the usual 16" stud spacing. Nothing in the plan addresses studs beyond 'drive into studs', and this is a wall-carried shelf.
Only two lag screws are listed for a load-bearing wall mount, and the plan does not say where along the frame they go.
COMPUTED: the three 3/4" plywood box bottoms total 30-1/2" of length against the 'at least 15-1/2" x 31"' panel the materials call for — 1/2" of margin before kerf.

Where the boxes sit vertically is set only by the rung positions, which are not dimensioned; the plan's own step 1 says to check the gap against a box before fastening, which is the only guidance available.
COMPUTED: the frame finishes 16-1/2" wide over the two 3/4" legs, which is exactly the 16-1/2" box front/back length — a clean internal check.

mantle-moulding-headboard.returnReason: 3-0 REJECT: step 7 nails a 22-3/4in back panel into the top AND bottom of a ~48-1/4in opening - impossible, under half the height, unflagged. v3 additionally proved a MISSED trim shortfall: 2x59-1/4 + 2x57 cannot pair from 84in lengths, so four 7ft are needed and three are supplied; the prose cleared it with a linear total.

mid-height-4-drawer-dresser-board-batten.returnReason: REJECT: step 3 fastens a 40-1/4in 2x2 to the inside of a 26-1/4in x 15-3/4in plywood side - fits neither dimension. Step 4 then claims two 15-3/4in members set a 40in inside width. The 40in shelf and 40in front trims prove the 40in pieces are the cross-case members step 3 misassigned.

mini-farmhouse-bedside-table.returnReason: 3-0 REJECT: step 6 sets the 10-1/2in fronts/backs BETWEEN the 11-1/4in sides, giving a 12in box for a 10-3/4in opening. The declared 9in x 11-1/4in drawer bottom proves the reverse order. Also step 4 installs all six 10-3/4in trims and step 8 fits the remaining ones - none remain.

flags: 1/4" PLYWOOD SHORTFALL, checked by area twice. The back panel (24" x 102-1/2" = 2,460 sq in) plus four drawer bottoms (23-3/4" x 26" = 2,470 sq in) total 4,930 sq in against 4,608 sq in in one 4x8 sheet. One sheet covers the drawer bottoms OR the back panel, not both. The back panel is conditional ('if the back is against a wall'), so this only bites on the walled version — but that is the version the plan describes first.

TWO PARTS ARE LONGER THAN THEIR STOCK: 'Top trim' is 1x3 at 102-1/2" and 'Front trim' is 1x6 at 102", but the only 1x3 and 1x6 listed are 8 ft (96") boards. Both must be spliced. Only the 1x2 retaining strip carries a 'can be spliced' note.

2x4, 8 ft IS EFFECTIVELY SHORT (checked by packing). Parts total 375" against 384"; the best packing found is 94-1/2" + 88-1/2" + 90" + 93", which leaves one 9" cross member unplaced, and the only arrangement that fits all ten requires three boards cut end to end at exactly 96" with no kerf. Budget a fifth 8 ft 2x4.

1x8 is similarly tight: 562-1/2" against 576", and only if four 24" pieces come out of each 96" board with no kerf. The cut list's own note says the cladding count is 'mix as needed to cover the arm ends', so treat 16 as approximate.

COMPUTED: each drawer opening is 23-3/4" wide (102-1/2" less five 1-1/2" cross members, divided by four), which is identical to the listed drawer width — side-mount slides need roughly 1" of total clearance, so no clearance is allowed for in the list.
The 'Drawer sides' (23-3/4") and 'Drawer fronts/backs' (26") labels are transposed against the 23-3/4" x 26" drawer bottom and the 26" slides.
COMPUTED: the four drawer faces total 101-1/2" across a 102-1/2" front, leaving about 1" of reveal to share out.
COMPUTED: fourteen 7-1/4" angled back boards is 101-1/2" against the 102-1/2" back frame — essentially edge to edge, with no gap to absorb cumulative error.
Seat slats are called for in the steps as 'scrap 1x boards' with no cut-list part and no material line.
The plywood material note says the sheet stock covers 'room walls, deck, and roof floor', but the cut list has NO roof-floor part.

Where the four 7-3/4" 1x4 room frame pieces sit — corner posts or a pair of end walls — is not stated, and the room's finished height and openness depend on which. The rewrite names both readings rather than picking one.
The roof peak trims are 11" while the roof frame's long sides are 14"; the plan does not say how the 3" difference is handled.

No gable pitch or roof overhang is given. COMPUTED: the 6-3/4" and 6" gable pieces differ by exactly 3/4", the moulding's own thickness, which is consistent with the two lapping at the peak rather than meeting point to point — but that only defines the lap, not the roof's rise over the 7-3/4" depth.
VERIFIED against the plan's own instruction: 2-1/2" spindles into 1/4"-deep holes top and bottom leave exactly 2" exposed, which matches the 2" corner posts. That one is fully self-consistent.
COMPUTED: one basic set uses about 148-1/2" of the 15 ft (180") of moulding listed; quantities here are explicitly per-module, so that is a one-set figure, not a total.
Spindle spacing along the railing is not given; twelve dowels are listed for a railing with 14" long sides and 7-3/4" short sides but no positions.

COMPUTED: 2x2 is very tight — eight 23-3/4" supports is 190" against the 192" in two 8 ft boards, and four per board is 95" of a 96" board before kerf. It fits, but a single bad cut costs you a support.
COMPUTED: 1x4 is tight — four 29-1/4" legs is 117" of the single 120" (10 ft) board listed, and the taper cuts leave no usable offcut.

The side aprons and the tabletop boards are both 88", so the top finishes flush at the ends with no overhang unless the legs are deliberately set in. The plan gives no leg inset dimension, so this is left as a decision with the consequence stated.
COMPUTED: the frame is 25-1/4" wide (23-3/4" end pieces plus the two 3/4" side aprons) under a 29" top (four 7-1/4" boards), giving about 1-7/8" of overhang on each long side.
COMPUTED, not authored: eight supports across the 88" run fall about every 12-1/2" on centres.
COMPUTED: finished height 30" (29-1/4" legs plus the 3/4" top).
'Simpson strong-tie corner brackets' are named in the final step but do not appear in materials[].
1x8, 1x3 and 1x6 all verified with margin: one 88" top board per 8 ft board; 164-1/2" of 192" for the 1x3; one 88" side apron plus one 23-3/4" end apron per 10 ft 1x6.

MATERIAL SHORTFALL (checked by packing, not just totals). 2x4: parts total 1,790-1/2" and need 21 eight-foot boards, because every 89" part takes its own 96" board (16 parts at 89", plus 2 boards for the 77-1/2" headers and 3 for the 70-1/2" cross beams); 12 are listed. 2x8: four 89" parts, four boards; 2 listed. 4x4: 8 boards needed (2 posts at 93", 2 boards for the four 37-1/2" short gable rafters at two per board, 4 boards for the four 58-1/4" long gable rafters); 6 listed. Only 2x6 is right: 13 decking + 3 braces = exactly the 16 boards supplied.
'Corner posts' quantity is 2, but the description says 'notched 4x4 corner posts' and the materials list 4 Simpson Strong-Tie post connectors. A 4-post structure needs 4 posts.
Every floor part is listed at 89" (outer frame, joists, rim boards). A butt-jointed frame needs the inner members roughly 3" shorter than the outer ones; as listed they cannot all be 89".
COMPUTED: 13 decking boards at 5-1/2" plus twelve 1/2" gaps covers 77-1/2", which does not match the 89" floor-frame parts (it does match the 77-1/2" header beam length).

Post and rafter notch dimensions exist nowhere in the plan ('as shown in the reference build'). The rewrite tells the builder to transfer each notch off its mating piece instead of inventing a number.
COMPUTED, not authored: a half-lap at the peak removes 1-3/4" from each mating piece (half the 4x4's listed 3-1/2" thickness).
The 5" lag screws named as a post-anchoring option in the steps are not in materials[].

DESCRIPTION CONTRADICTS ITSELF: it says 'the bottom step is built from 2x8s while the top step also uses 2x8s, which makes the bottom step 1-1/2" taller than the top'. Two boxes of the same stock are the same height; the height difference only exists if one box is built from different stock (which is what the cut-list note on 'Bottom step, ends' offers).

COMPUTED: 2x8 parts total 546" against 576" supplied (six 8 ft boards) and they do pack into six boards — but only because three parts are used at the full 96" length of a board, leaving no allowance to trim a damaged end. Not a shortfall; a tightness worth knowing.
The eight 'Top trim' pieces are all 22-1/2" with both ends mitered at 45°, which forms two closed 22-1/2" square frames. The plan never says where on the 96" run those two frames sit.
'Bottom step, ends' quantity 5 and 'Top step, ends' quantity 4 imply internal dividers inside each box; the plan never states this.

COMPUTED, not authored: bottom box finishes 96" long by 21" deep (18" ends plus the two 1-1/2" long sides). Verified 2x4 (318-1/2" of 384") and 2x6 (216" of 240") both pack comfortably — no shortfall in either.

CONTRADICTION (blocking): the sink top frame cannot hold the stated sink. Two 18" front/back pieces with two 9-3/4" sides captured between them leaves an interior opening of 15" x 9-3/4", but the material list calls for a sink 'about 16" x 10-3/4"'. The plan already says to measure your own sink and adjust, so the rewrite leads with that rather than quoting an opening the sink won't fit.

MATERIAL SHORTFALL: the two backs are 18" x 19-1/4" and 15" x 19-1/4" — 33" of combined width — plus the door back panels, all from one piece of 1/4" plywood listed as '2 ft x 2 ft plus scrap for door backs'. A 24" x 24" piece cannot yield both backs.

TOOL GAP: no sheet-goods saw (circular, table or jig) is in tools[], yet two 1/4" plywood backs and the door back panels have to be cut from a sheet. The cut step names only the miter saw, which cannot make those cuts.
CONTRADICTION: the hardware count implies three doors (6 surface mount hinges = 3 pairs, 3 magnetic clasps) while the summary and description describe one door per unit, i.e. two.
Under-specified (deliberate per the plan): the door frames have no cut-list entry at all — they are sized to the actual opening. Flagged so a verifier does not read it as an omission by the rewrite.

AMBIGUOUS SOURCE WORDING: the original step says the interior shelf leaves 'about 6" of space under the bottom shelf', which does not parse against the geometry (the bottom shelf sits on the toekick). The rewrite repeats the plan's 6" figure without reinterpreting it.

DERIVED: case widths. Sink 16-1/2" bottom + two 3/4" sides = 18", matching the 18" front and back panel; stove 13-1/2" + 1-1/2" = 15", matching its 15" parts. Sink top frame outer size 18" x 11-1/4" (9-3/4" + two 3/4" rails), matching the 11-1/4" case depth.

printers-console-table.returnReason: 2-1 confirm OVERRIDDEN, highest severity in the batch: the shelf "correction" is BACKWARDS and instructs a wrong cut on a CORRECT cut list. The 58in x 33in back panel proves the 33in sides run full height and flank the top and bottom (56-1/2 + 3/4 + 3/4 = 58), so the openings are 27-7/8in - exactly the listed shelf. Step 8 tells the builder each shelf is 3/4in too long and to cut 27-1/8in, leaving both loose. Steps 9 and 10 propagate the same error.

rolling-room-divider-cubbies.returnReason: 2-1 confirm OVERRIDDEN: step 7 declares the correct 14in trim arbitrary and tells the builder to re-cut every piece. 14in is exact - the 48in case less two 1-1/2in side trims and two 1-1/2in divider-column trims leaves 42in over three cubbies. Both lumber shortfalls in this plan ARE real and were proved by hand.

flags: MISSING CUT-LIST ENTRY + MATERIAL SHORTFALL: step 4 tells you to attach 'back support strips' from the 1x2, but there is no cut-list row for them. The 10 ft (120") 1x2 is already consumed by the 45" bottom trim, the 48" top front trim and two 9-1/4" side trims = 111-1/2", leaving 8-1/2". There is not enough 1x2 for back supports of any useful length.
DERIVED (exact): three cubbies across the 45" bottom shelf with two 3/4" dividers = 14-1/2" each. The plan gives no divider spacing.

DERIVED: the 8-1/2" dividers run between the top and the bottom shelf, so the bottom shelf hangs 8-1/2" below the underside of the top and the remaining roughly 21" of the 30-1/4" sides is the open bay beneath. The plan never states the shelf height.

DERIVED (exact, corroborating): the 48" top front trim = the case's 46-1/2" width plus a 3/4" side trim at each end. And the 31" back panel height = 30-1/4" sides + the 3/4" top, which is why the rewrite uses the panel as a square check.
Note, not a defect: the 1x10 stock is sufficient — 169" of parts from two 8 ft boards, and it packs (46-1/2" + 30-1/4" + two 8-1/2" on one board, 45" + 30-1/4" on the other).

COMPUTED: the tabletop is 17-1/2" x 18-1/2" (five 3-1/2" boards by 18-1/2") over a frame that is 15" x 16", which gives an even 1-1/4" overhang on all four sides. That is the only reading of the cut list where the overhang comes out even, and the description does say the even overhang matters — but the number itself is computed, not authored.
COMPUTED: finished height 17" (16-1/4" legs plus the 3/4" top).
'Frame long sides' quantity is 3 for a four-sided frame. The third is read here as a center rail for the top boards to land on; the plan never says so.
The original steps call for glue at the frame joints, but no glue appears in materials[]. Prose reworded to avoid specifying a material the shopping list does not carry.
COMPUTED: 1x4 yield is tight — five 18-1/2" boards is 92-1/2" of a 96" board before saw kerf. The 1x3 (139" of 192") packs comfortably.
No sander is in tools[], so the finishing step is hand sanding by implication.

simple-planked-wood-bed.returnReason: 3-0 REJECT: step 2 invents a conflict - 33in is the panel HEIGHT, the planks are 31-3/4in LONG and match the rail and leg blocks exactly. Telling a beginner to settle a phantom 1-1/4in at the bench means ripping a correct plank. Also one of two declared leg blocks unused, and step 4 drops the footboard panel the description calls for.

flags: DERIVED (exact arithmetic, not authored): slat gaps. Ten 3-1/2" top slats across a 39-1/2" rail leaves 4-1/2" over nine gaps = exactly 1/2". Nine shelf slats across a 35-1/2" rail leaves 4" over eight gaps = exactly 1/2". Stated as 1/2" in the prose.

DERIVED: finished top is 39-1/2" x 16" (9" slat + a 3-1/2" edge rail each side) and the apron frame is 35-1/2" x 14" (33" + two 1-1/4" legs; 11-1/2" + two 1-1/4" legs), giving a 2" end and 1" side overhang. Consistent throughout the cut list but never written down in the plan.
Under-specified: the height of the bottom apron / lower shelf above the floor is given nowhere. The rewrite says to set it where you want it rather than inventing a number.
Under-specified: notch size for the shelf rails. The 1-1/4" square baluster dimension is in the material name, so the rewrite states 1-1/4" and tells the builder to mark off the actual leg.

Data gap, not fixed here: the plan is an outdoor piece with a random-orbit-sander listed as an essential tool, but no finish, sealer or exterior stain appears in materials[] and no step used the sander. The rewrite adds sanding to the final step and stops there — adding a finish line is a materials change, out of scope for a step rewrite.

toddler-chalkboard-easel.returnReason: REJECT: step 6 INVENTS a defect and discards a correct dimension. By its own assembly two 3/4in sides on a 23in bottom give 24-1/2in outside - the tray front is exactly right. Telling the builder to re-cut it to what they built yields a front that no longer covers the side ends, and breaks step 7, which derives the dowel projection FROM the 24-1/2in it just abandoned. The 15in tray sides on a 3-1/2in tray IS a real anomaly; bundling the sound front into it is the error.

flags: Material sufficiency (dowels): the cut list needs six 18" and six 12" dowels = 180" from four 48" dowels = 192". On paper it fits, but no cutting pattern yields it cleanly: two 18" pieces per stick leaves an 11-7/8" offcut once kerf is taken, i.e. an eighth under a 12" front dowel. Three of the six front dowels end up marginally short. Surfaced in the cut step rather than written around.
DERIVED, not authored: dowel spacing. Six dowels evenly along a 30" board works out to roughly 5" on center with the first about 2-1/2" from each end. The plan specifies no spacing.
DERIVED: screw count. 12 dowel screws + 2 screws per corner x 4 corners = 20, exactly the 20 screws supplied. No spares.

Under-specified: how the two 14" end pieces register to the 30" long sides (butted on the outside faces vs. let in between) is not stated anywhere, so the rack's overall footprint is not determined by the data. The rewrite picks flush-to-the-outside and says so as a choice, not a measurement.
DERIVED: exposed dowel length = cut length less the 3/4" socket depth (17-1/4" back, 11-1/4" front). Not stated in the plan; not written into the prose.
The original step 3 called for '2" finish nails', but the only nail in materials[] is 'Brad nails, 1-1/4"'. Prose corrected to the listed material.

COMPUTED, not authored: the trim lengths are internally consistent — 15-1/2" side trim = the 15-1/4" case depth plus the 1/4" back; 41-1/2" front trim = the 40" case plus the two 3/4" side trims; 38-1/2" shelves = 40" less the two 3/4" sides; 29-3/4" back height = the 29" sides plus the 3/4" top.
Shelf height is not specified anywhere. The cut list gives two shelves and two sides but no position for the upper shelf — builder's choice.
Yields verified, no shortfall: the 3/4" sheet ripped to 15-1/4" yields three 96" strips (288") against 175" of parts; 1x2 and 1x3 each need 111" of the 192" supplied.

MATERIAL SHORTFALL: the small tree's four 1x4 parts — base 4-1/4", layers 10-1/8", 7-1/8" and 4" — total 25-1/2", but the material list supplies a 2 ft (24") board. Short by 1-1/2" before saw kerf. Surfaced in the cut step. (The large tree is fine: 39" of parts from a 4 ft board.)

DATA GAP vs. TITLE: the plan is called a candle holder, but neither the cut list nor materials[] contains a candle cup, a bored recess, or any fireproof insert, and no step drills one. The rewrite does not invent a hole size; the final step says to use a proper holder on the base and names the hazard.

DERIVED from the cut list note: 'both ends cut 30° off square, ends not parallel' means each layer is a wedge — the two miters run the same way rather than mirroring, so the ends splay outward from the long-point edge. The rewrite describes that orientation; the plan states the angle and the non-parallel condition but not which edge is long.

Under-specified: the layers' vertical stacking positions (how much of each layer shows above the one below) are not given. The rewrite uses the back piece as the registration and does not invent a spacing.

DRAWER PARTS DO NOT RECONCILE. 'Drawer face' is 44" and 'Drawer bottom' is 16" x 44", and the description says the drawer is built 1" narrower than the opening (the aprons are 45") — three sources agreeing on a wide, shallow drawer spanning the desk front. Against that, 'Drawer front/back' is listed at 12". The rewrite takes width from the measured opening and flags the 12" figure rather than declaring either reading correct.
Drawer depth is also ambiguous: sides are 15-1/4" but the bottom is 16" deep — a 3/4" mismatch whichever way the front/back cap the sides.

COMPUTED: 1x4 yield is tight. Two 24" side aprons plus both 45" aprons total 138" from one 12 ft (144") board, and the aprons are cut at 35° so the long-point lengths eat more than the nominal. Skipping the front apron for the drawer version drops it to 93".
The desktop is used at the panel's full 24" x 48" — no trim allowance at all.
The 23-1/8" bottom spread is stated in the description and step 4 but no cut-list part sets it directly; it is a check dimension only.
COMPUTED: 1x3 parts total 442-3/4" against 576" (six 8 ft boards) and pack into five — comfortable, no shortfall.
Where the side apron sits vertically on each leg assembly is not dimensioned; the rewrite gives the registration (35° end flat against the leg) rather than a number.
